title: Recommendation: Expand automated evals cautiously (phased), with stronger gating + clustering first
summary: Proceed with expansion only after one more smaller, highly-instrumented batch that makes failure patterns visible and enforces a citation-support shipping gate. The last broad run increased scored cases but reduced reviewer efficiency and pattern detection; cost also rose due to uncontrolled retries. A phased approach preserves trace/debug value while controlling cost and overfitting risk.

claims.text: Broad eval runs increased the number of scored cases but made it harder for reviewers to distinguish repeated failure patterns from one-off mistakes; smaller batches with visible clusters produced more actionable changes.
Coverage can improve while grounding/citation support regresses, so citation support should be an explicit release/shipping gate rather than a leaderboard target.

Long traces are useful for debugging, but unchecked retries caused a 1.34x cost increase; finance will accept trace retention if retries are capped and summarized in a run manifest (retry count, estimated cost, and what the trace resolved).
Holdout cases should be visible only as summaries/aggregate scores in dashboards; labels/expected answers should be excluded from prompt-iteration workflows to reduce memorization/overfitting risk.

Reviewers want dashboards that separately cluster unsupported claims, weak citations, cost regressions, and holdout concerns; they prefer fewer cases with clearer evidence over large runs requiring manual interpretation per row.

openQuestions: What exact citation-support threshold (block condition) will be used for the shipping gate, and how will ‘unsupported claim’ be operationally detected and clustered?
What retry cap will be set, and what is the acceptable cost budget for an eval run?
What dashboard clustering taxonomy and UX will be implemented to make failure patterns ‘visible’ in the next batch (e.g., grouping by unsupported-claim pattern vs weak-citation pattern)?
What size constitutes the next ‘smaller visible batch,’ and what criteria will trigger expansion to broader coverage after it?
recommendation: Do not immediately expand to broader automated coverage. Run one more smaller, reviewer-friendly batch first, with: (1) a hard citation-support shipping gate tracked separately from coverage, (2) a dashboard that clusters failures by pattern (unsupported claims/weak citations/cost/holdout signals), (3) retained long traces but with capped retries and a manifest summarizing retry count and estimated cost, and (4) holdout performance reported only in aggregate (no tuning labels in prompt-iteration tools). Expand automated eval coverage only if the next candidate improves grounding while staying within the cost guardrail.
